title: Greek Spinach Rice

subs.name: Frozen spinach (palak) works perfectly — thaw and squeeze out excess water before using; baby spinach needs no chopping
Any white rice works; avoid basmati for the most authentic texture as it tends to stay more separate rather than creamy
Good quality sunflower or groundnut oil works; a small knob of butter added at the end gives richness in place of olive oil
Fresh mint or coriander leaves give a different but equally aromatic result; dried dill can be used in half the quantity

steps.text: Heat the olive oil in a wide kadai or heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the onion and cook, stirring occasionally, for 5–6 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the garlic and cook for another minute until it smells fragrant.

Add the chopped tomato if using and cook for 3 minutes until it softens. Add the spinach in two or three handfuls, stirring and allowing each addition to wilt before adding the next — the volume will reduce dramatically within a minute or two.

Add the rice and stir to coat the grains in the oily spinach mixture. Pour in the water or stock, season generously with salt, and bring to a boil. Stir once, then reduce the heat to low, cover tightly, and cook for 15 minutes without lifting the lid.

After 15 minutes, check if the rice has absorbed all the liquid. If any liquid remains, cook uncovered for 2–3 more minutes. Remove from heat, squeeze the lemon juice over the top, scatter the dill, and stir gently to combine.

Cover and rest off the heat for 5 minutes before serving — this allows the rice to finish steaming and the dill to release its flavour into the dish. Serve warm with extra lemon wedges and a drizzle of olive oil, and optionally crumble feta cheese over the top (skip for vegan).

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

substitution_notes: spinach → Frozen spinach (palak) works perfectly — thaw and squeeze out excess water before using; baby spinach needs no chopping; dill → Fresh mint or coriander leaves give a different but equally aromatic result; dried dill can be used in half the quantity; rice → Any white rice works; avoid basmati for the most authentic texture as it tends to stay more separate rather than creamy; olive-oil → Good quality sunflower or groundnut oil works; a small knob of butter added at the end gives richness in place of olive oil
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Palak (spinach) is abundantly available and inexpensive across India — frozen palak works too. Any rice from your pantry (even sona masoori) produces a good result. Fresh dill (shepu or suva) is sold in bunches at many Indian vegetable markets; mint (pudina) is a good substitute. A small kadai with a tight-fitting lid makes the one-pot method effortless. The dish eats like a mildly seasoned palak pulao and appeals to children and adults alike.
